description: Explains how Craft turns player input into movement, view changes, block editing, signs, chat, slash commands, hit testing, and collision behavior.
document:
Craft's gameplay loop is built around direct GLFW input callbacks and a per-frame movement update. The README presents the player-facing model: keyboard controls move, jump, fly, zoom, switch views, select block types, enter chat or commands, and write sign text, while mouse buttons destroy blocks, create blocks, toggle lights, or pick an existing block type. In `main.c`, those controls are split between event handlers for discrete actions and `handle_movement`, which continuously reads movement and camera keys each frame. Typing mode is an important boundary: while the player is composing chat, a command, or sign text, normal movement and item-selection shortcuts are suppressed so text input does not also edit the world.

The keyboard and character callbacks cooperate to manage text entry. `on_char` starts a blank chat buffer for the chat key, starts a slash-prefixed buffer for commands, or starts a sign-prefixed buffer for block text. `on_key` edits that buffer with backspace, allows a shifted enter to insert a line break, and submits on ordinary enter. Submitted sign text is attached to the face the player is looking at, slash-prefixed text goes to command parsing, and other text is sent as chat. Escape cancels typing; outside typing, it releases the captured cursor. Clipboard paste appends to the active text buffer, but when the player is not typing it is treated as command text that is parsed locally without being forwarded.

Block interaction uses sight-based hit testing rather than a separate selection object. `hit_test` scans a ray from the player's eye through nearby chunks and returns the closest solid block along the sight vector. When asked for the previous position, it reports the empty cell just before that block, which is how right-click placement chooses the build location. Left-click destruction requires a destructible target within the allowed vertical range, removes the block, records the change, and also clears a plant directly above it. Right-click placement uses the selected item from the buildable item list, refuses to place inside the player's two-block body, and records the change. A control-modified right-click toggles a target block as a light source, while middle-click searches the buildable item list for the looked-at block and selects it.

Movement combines keyboard intent, camera direction, gravity, and collision correction. The per-frame movement handler reads forward, backward, strafe, jump, flight, zoom, orthographic view, and arrow-key look input when the user is not typing. It turns the movement request into a motion vector, uses faster movement while flying than while walking, and subdivides each frame into multiple small steps so collision has several chances to correct the position. `collide` checks nearby block cells for obstacles and nudges the player away from them with a small padding distance; vertical contacts report a hit so the jump velocity can be reset. The obstacle test in `item.c` deliberately excludes empty space, clouds, and plants, so the player can pass through those but not ordinary terrain blocks.

Slash commands are handled by `parse_command`, which recognizes local commands for login state, online/offline mode, viewing distance, clipboard-like builder selection, tree generation, arrays, cubes, spheres, circles, and cylinders. The README also documents user-facing multiplayer commands such as going to another user, listing connected users, and returning to spawn; in the client parser, any slash line not handled locally is forwarded through chat when forwarding is enabled, allowing the server side of multiplayer to handle commands that are not local building or mode changes.

Signs are stored separately from ordinary blocks. The backquote entry path uses `hit_test_face` to find both the target block and the face being viewed, then `set_sign` stores the text in the chunk's `SignList`, writes it to the database, and sends it to the client networking layer. The sign list implementation replaces existing text on the same block face before appending the new entry, grows its array as needed, and can remove either one face or all signs for a block. Empty sign text removes that face instead of storing a blank sign.
